why_correct: The Claude API is stateless by design — there is no server-side session object accumulating conversation history between requests. Each API call is a self-contained inference: the model sees exactly what you send in the messages array and nothing else. To support multi-turn coherence, the orchestration layer must reconstruct the full conversation history (all prior human and assistant turns) and include it in every subsequent request. The failure symptom — follow-ups that ignore prior context — is the canonical signature of missing message history in the payload.

distractors.A: The system prompt controls persona, constraints, and behavioral framing, not what conversational history the model can access. A longer system prompt doesn't inject prior turns into the context window. A would be relevant if Claude were ignoring instructions, not ignoring prior user messages.
distractors.C: There is no session token or server-side conversational state to expire — that's the entire point. This misconception imports a stateful-session mental model (think WebSocket or database session) onto a stateless HTTP inference API. In production, chasing session expiry bugs here wastes time because the mechanism doesn't exist.
distractors.D: Modern Claude models have context windows large enough to hold many turns of conversation. The failure occurs at zero turns of history being sent, not at window saturation. Window exhaustion is a real concern in long sessions, but it produces degraded or truncated recall, not immediate nonsense on the second message.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: Claude has no memory between API calls unless you give it memory. Every multi-turn implementation is responsible for maintaining and resending the full messages array. If follow-ups fail immediately, the first diagnostic is always 'what does the actual request payload look like?' — not model capability or configuration.
cross_domain_note: This directly ties to cost and token budget management: resending full history on every turn means token consumption scales with conversation length, which makes prompt caching a high-value optimization for long-running support sessions.

why_correct: The append-only pattern creates linear token growth on the input side — max_tokens controls output length, not how many tokens the model can receive. Once accumulated history exceeds the context window, the API returns an error (outright failures) and, just before that threshold, the model is forced to attend over a degraded, truncated prefix — which explains the contradictions. Periodic summarization is the canonical remedy: compress settled context into a durable summary node, retain a recent verbatim window for coherence, and you convert O(n) token growth into O(1) amortized cost per session.

distractors.A: Temperature controls sampling stochasticity, not context fidelity. Lowering it to 0.2 narrows the output distribution but does nothing about the fact that the model is receiving a truncated or overflowing input — it will still contradict itself because the early guidance is simply no longer in the context window. Temperature tuning is the right lever for factual drift caused by high-variance sampling; this is a token-budget problem, not a sampling problem.
distractors.C: Rate-limit errors surface as 429s with a retry-after header and are session-agnostic — they don't produce within-session coherence drift, and they don't correlate with session length the way the 90-minute threshold does. The architectural tell is the time-correlated failure pattern: rate limits would hit high-concurrency bursts uniformly, not long sessions specifically. Retry logic is the right remedy for transient 429s, not for context overflow.
distractors.D: The append-only pattern isn't the problem — unbounded growth of that list is. Forcing a hard reset every 30 minutes destroys semantic continuity and pushes state management complexity onto users, which is the wrong trust boundary for a legal research tool. Session restart is a UX failure mode, not an architectural pattern.

mental_model: When you see time-correlated failures in a messages-list architecture, always check whether history length is crossing the context window before reaching for any other diagnosis. The decision rule: input overflow → summarization; output truncation → max_tokens; transient errors → retry with backoff. These are three distinct failure modes with non-overlapping remedies.
cross_domain_note: This directly intersects cost optimization: unbounded history also means unbounded prompt tokens on every call. Summarization compresses both the reliability risk and the per-call token cost — cache-hit rate also improves because a stable prepended summary is more likely to match a cached prefix than an ever-growing verbatim history.

why_correct: The Anthropic API is stateless — there is no server-side session memory. Every request must carry the full conversation history in the messages array for Claude to have any context. Hardcoding messages=[] in the chat() function body means the accumulated list is built correctly in memory but never passed to the API, so every turn is effectively a cold start. The symptom — Claude responding as if the prior exchange never happened — is the exact production signature of a stateless API receiving an empty context window.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is assuming the model has implicit memory of its own outputs. Claude has no out-of-band access to prior assistant turns; the assistant role messages in the history array are the only mechanism by which Claude 'knows' what it previously said. Omitting assistant turns produces incoherent follow-ups and broken referential chains like the one in the stem.
distractors.C: This is a plausible Python gotcha — list mutation vs. rebinding — but the stem says the developer 'correctly appends' to the messages list, ruling out a reference aliasing bug. If C were the cause, history would be lost silently on the add step; here, history is built correctly but never passed to the API call, which is a different failure point entirely.
distractors.D: The Anthropic API has no session_id parameter. This conflates a stateful session model (think WebSocket or server-side conversation store) with the actual stateless request/response contract. Any architect who has read the API spec rules this out immediately.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for multi-turn correctness: trace the messages object from construction to the API call site. If the accumulated list and the object passed to the API are not the same reference, context is silently dropped regardless of how correctly the history is maintained. Always ask 'what is actually in the messages array at call time?' before debugging anything else.
cross_domain_note: This pattern reappears in agentic loops — an orchestration layer that maintains tool call history locally but instantiates a fresh messages payload per LLM call will exhibit the same amnesiac behavior, making it a common failure mode in multi-step reasoning pipelines.

why_correct: The Messages API expects a strictly alternating user/assistant turn structure — consecutive user-role messages break the conversational thread at the protocol level. When assistant turns are discarded, Claude has no grounding in its own prior output, so each follow-up is effectively a cold-start query against fragmented user utterances. The contradictions aren't a coherence drift problem or a token-budget problem — they're a structural context absence problem: Claude literally cannot see what it previously concluded, so it reasons from scratch each turn.

distractors.A: Near-miss. Context window pressure is a real failure mode in long sessions, but it requires the history to actually be present before recency bias can kick in. Here the assistant turns were never appended at all — there's no growing list to overflow. A would be the right diagnosis if the team was sending full history but attorneys were losing coherence only in sessions exceeding ~20+ turns.
distractors.C: This is the summarization anti-pattern — it confuses a token-budget optimization with a correctness fix. Summarizing responses before storage is a valid strategy for compressing long histories, but it's irrelevant when the root cause is that assistant turns are discarded entirely. Applying summarization here would still produce the same contradiction failures.
distractors.D: The system prompt is stateless scaffolding — behavioral framing, not conversational memory. Re-sending it correctly on every request (which is standard practice) has no bearing on whether Claude can reference its own prior reasoning. Conflating behavioral context with conversational context is the core error.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: if the symptom is self-contradiction or failure to build on prior reasoning, audit the assistant turns first. Claude can only ground follow-up answers in prior output that exists in the messages array — it has no implicit session memory. Missing assistant turns = stateless reasoning on every turn, regardless of how many user messages are present.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic loop design: in tool-use pipelines, discarding intermediate tool-result or assistant messages produces the same failure mode — the model re-derives conclusions it already reached because its own reasoning trace isn't visible in subsequent iterations.

why_correct: The Messages API requires strict user/assistant role alternation to reconstruct conversational state. When add_assistant_message() is skipped, the messages list becomes a sequence of consecutive user-role entries — Claude never sees its own prior responses, so from its perspective there's no prior exchange to reference, only a growing pile of user utterances with no coherent turn structure. By turn three, the missing assistant turns mean Claude has never been given the grounding of its own prior outputs, which is precisely what multi-turn coherence depends on. The architectural principle: the conversation history IS the memory — if the assistant turns aren't in the list, they don't exist.

distractors.B: What makes B tempting is that a missing system prompt can cause behavioral drift, and 'resetting conversational framing' sounds plausible. But the system prompt is optional — its absence affects persona and instruction anchoring, not Claude's ability to track conversation history. The failure mode described (contextual isolation by turn three) is a memory/context problem, not a behavioral-anchor problem. B would produce inconsistent tone or policy adherence, not amnesia.
distractors.C: This is the silent-truncation misconception — the idea that the API enforces some undocumented role-uniformity constraint and quietly drops messages. No such threshold exists. The API will surface a validation error on malformed role sequences in some cases, but it does not silently truncate based on role uniformity. In production, C would manifest as explicit API errors or empty responses, not the gradual context loss the QA team observed.
distractors.D: Mutation-in-place would produce a different failure signature: every turn would appear to have only the most recent user message, making Claude context-blind from turn one, not turn three. The stem's detail that 'logging confirms the full messages list is being sent' directly rules this out — if the list were being overwritten, the log would show a single-entry list, not a growing one.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for multi-turn context bugs: if Claude progressively loses context rather than having no context from the start, the messages list is growing but structurally malformed — missing assistant turns is the first thing to check. Role alternation isn't just convention; it's how the model reconstructs who said what.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic loop design: in tool-use sequences, skipping the assistant turn that contains tool_use blocks before injecting tool_result entries produces the same structural corruption — the model loses track of what it requested, causing cascading reasoning failures.

why_correct: The core failure here is a trust boundary violation: users externalize memory to the assistant and stop maintaining it themselves. Budget ceilings and scope decisions are stated once precisely because the user believes the system has durable retention — they're not pleasantries, they're the session's ground truth. Hard truncation removes these silently with no error signal, so the assistant continues generating responses that appear coherent but contradict committed constraints, and neither the user nor the system knows it. The correct pattern for sessions with non-recoverable early-turn constraints is entity extraction and persistent structured state — pull budget, scope, stakeholder decisions into a sidecar store at ingestion time so truncation only affects the dialogue surface, not the semantic anchors.

distractors.A: The cost and latency framing is real but secondary — bursty truncation overhead is an operational nuisance, not a correctness failure. A would be the dominant concern if the sessions were stateless or if users were re-stating context each turn. The architectural tell in the stem is 'regularly referencing decisions stated early' — that's a correctness risk, not a cost-smoothing problem.
distractors.C: This is the right shape of concern but wrong session profile. Tool-call result loss matters in agentic loops where mid-session structured outputs gate later actions. The stem describes a productivity assistant with human decision-making turns, not a multi-step tool-use chain. If the session pattern were agentic with dependent function-call results, C would be the answer to chase.
distractors.D: The 80k threshold placement is an implementation detail, not the architectural risk. Even a perfectly tuned threshold doesn't fix silent constraint loss — it just changes which messages get dropped.

mental_model: When users externalize memory to a conversational system, truncation is no longer just a token-budget decision — it's a data-loss decision. The CCA heuristic: any message containing a user-committed constraint (budget, scope, decision) is non-recoverable via re-prompt and must be extracted to persistent state before it enters the truncation window. Hard sliding windows are only safe when all early-turn content is re-derivable.
cross_domain_note: This is the conversational analog of the RAG grounding problem: just as you wouldn't rely on in-context document retention across sessions, you shouldn't rely on in-context constraint retention across a long session's truncation boundary — both require an explicit retrieval or state-persistence layer.

why_correct: Omitting max_tokens hands the model a blank check on the token budget — API defaults are designed for generality, not cost or latency SLA compliance. The correct pattern for a shared helper serving heterogeneous features is parameterization with a conservative default: the helper enforces a safe ceiling, while each call site owns its own generation budget appropriate to its output contract. This decouples the shared infrastructure concern (cost guardrails) from the per-feature concern (expected response length), which is exactly what makes it scale across five distinct use cases. The status summarizer's p95 latency spike is the canonical symptom of over-generation on a low-output task — the model is burning tokens the feature never needed.

distractors.A: Streaming fixes perceived latency by surfacing tokens earlier, but it does not reduce the number of tokens generated — the generation budget is identical. The status summarizer's latency problem is over-generation, not display lag. Streaming is the right answer when the problem is time-to-first-token or progressive rendering UX; it's orthogonal to cost and total generation length.
distractors.C: Hardcoding 4096 globally is the one-size-fits-none anti-pattern. It may over-provision the status summarizer (still burning excess tokens) while silently truncating the weekly digest or brainstorming assistant mid-output. In production, this breaks under any feature whose correct output exceeds the cap or whose correct output is far below it — you've just moved the budget problem rather than solved it.
distractors.D: Temperature controls sampling stochasticity, not output length. Lowering temperature produces more deterministic token selection but does not reduce how many tokens the model generates before hitting a stop condition. This is a persistent misconception — the failure mode is that costs remain elevated while response diversity collapses, giving you the worst of both outcomes.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for shared infrastructure over heterogeneous call sites: push variation to the call site, enforce safe defaults at the helper boundary. Whenever you see a single helper serving features with wildly different output-length contracts, missing per-call-site max_tokens is always the architectural gap — cost and latency SLAs cannot be met without it.
cross_domain_note: This same parameterization pattern applies to prompt caching strategy: a shared helper that cache-keys on full context needs call-site awareness of which prefix segments are stable enough to cache, or you'll see cache miss rates that vary as unpredictably as the token costs did here.

why_correct: The core principle is that max_tokens is a workflow contract, not a helper-level constant — it encodes how much output a specific call site expects, and that expectation varies structurally across workflows. Making max_tokens a parameter with a sensible default preserves backward compatibility while letting each call site own its budget. This is the standard 'configurable with opinionated default' pattern for shared infrastructure: the helper stays reusable, but policy moves to the caller where the semantic context lives.

distractors.A: The failure mode is assuming Claude self-limits — it doesn't. Claude will consume up to max_tokens if the task warrants it, so setting 4096 globally means triage calls may produce verbose multi-paragraph verdicts when a single sentence was the UX contract. Raising a shared ceiling fixes runbooks but breaks triage behavior and wastes token budget on every short-form call. A would be appropriate only if all three workflows had identical output-length profiles.
distractors.B: The misconception is that the API has dynamic, context-aware allocation. The API default is a fixed value (typically 1024 or model-specific), completely indifferent to conversation length or workflow semantics. Removing max_tokens doesn't give you 'more headroom as context grows' — it just substitutes one wrong constant for another, and you lose explicit budget control entirely. In production, this makes token budgeting invisible and non-auditable.
distractors.D: Resubmitting with a truncated assistant turn appended is the continuation anti-pattern — it looks like it recovers, but it corrupts the assistant turn boundary. Claude now sees a partial assistant message as prior context and will try to complete it, which produces structural incoherence in runbook formatting and can cause role-alternation violations depending on how the messages list is assembled. Fix the budget at the source; don't paper over it downstream.

mental_model: Whenever a shared helper hardcodes a generation parameter that has semantic meaning per call site, the fix is always parameterization with a default — never a global raise or removal. The architectural tell is 'three workflows, one constant' — that's a signal the abstraction boundary is in the wrong place.
cross_domain_note: This directly intersects with prompt caching strategy: if you're passing workflow-specific max_tokens per call site, you're already thinking per-call-site, which is also where you'd pin cache breakpoints. Mismatched max_tokens and cache boundaries are a common source of unexpected cache misses on high-traffic multi-turn systems.
